Here the carnivorous growth has won outright: the floor is a mat of root and rot studded with wide, waiting blooms, and the bones of what they have caught lie half-dissolved among the stems — a picked ribcage, the emptied shell of a scorpion, one boot. Sap strings the air, sweetish and rank enough to taste. The thorny mouths turn toward the warmth of you as you pass, slow as sunflowers tracking a sun, and the fronds settle closed behind you soft as a held breath. Nothing here gives chase; nothing here has ever needed to. The choke lies back north; a gap in the growth falls away east toward a wider dark.

d7.gloaming.pools.name: The Scalded Steps
d7.gloaming.pools.description: East off the landing the shelf steps down into water again — a fall of terraced pools deeper and hotter than any above, brimming and green-lit and breathing scalding steam up into the dark. The growth thins where the heat is fiercest: only a grey crust of dried salt and a few blistered ferns hold the ledges, and the slick stone between them is treacherous, a scald waiting on a careless foot. Flat pale scorpions run the hot rock where little else will, out for the basking and the wounded alike. The landing lies back west; south, the steps run down toward a wider, choked hollow where the light gathers.

d7.gloaming.hollow.name: The Choked Hollow
d7.gloaming.hollow.description: The ways converge in a wide hollow where the canopy lifts and opens and the cold-green light pools bright — the one true glow this deep, and every hungry thing in the lower gulf knows it by heart. The growth ranks thick around the light's edge — moss and fern and low fronds hung heavy with emberfruit — and among them the odd too-fleshy bloom that is no fruit at all, and something is always waiting in it: the light lures the midges, the midges draw the grazers, and the grazers draw whatever is patient enough to stand still in the green and let its food come walking in. Ways run back north to the scalded steps and west into the snare; east a low gap breathes silk and warm water, and at the hollow's far lip the floor gives way downward into a sodden dark.

d7.gloaming.weave.name: The Weeping Weave
d7.gloaming.weave.description: The east gap opens into a dripping bay where the Weeping Chasm-Moss has run riot — a whole wall and roof of it, the richest curtain of the stuff a prospector could dream of, its hair-fine threads hanging in their thousands and glowing a soft blue-white through the steam. It is a fortune in gloom-silk for the pulling. It is also exactly the kind of quiet, generous, easy place the Gloaming keeps its teeth in, and the moss is not the only thing here that has grown patient and still and glad of the warmth. The hollow lies back west.

d7.gloaming.maw.name: The Sodden Maw
d7.gloaming.maw.description: Down from the hollow the growth gives out at last. The canopy cannot reach here; the ferns dwindle to sickly threads and then to nothing, and the light dies with them into a breathing black. This is the foot of the green — a sodden throat of the gulf where the warm water pours away downward into a dark that has never been lit, and the smell rising out of it is no longer the smell of growing things. A line has been rigged at the rim since, and a stake driven; someone went down into the reek and the heat, whatever the Gloaming was the pleasant face of, and left the rigging behind them. The hollow climbs back up toward the light; the line pays out down into the boiling dark below.

exitMessages.down: You take the rigged line and lower yourself over the rim into the reek. The warm water sheets down the rock beside you and the last of the green dies out overhead; the air turns thick and scalding, the stone underhand goes slick and then hot, and the steady drip becomes a hiss becomes a low boiling roar — until your boots find a drowned stair in the steam and you step off the line into the seething foot of the Gloaming, a full stage below the green.

d8.scald.stair.name: The Drowned Stair
d8.scald.stair.description: The line sets you down on a worked stair — someone cut these steps, long ago, and the deep has been unmaking them ever since. Warm water sheets down them in a thin scalding film and steam stands in the air thick enough to drink, so that the little light there is comes from nowhere and shows nothing past arm's reach. The false sky is gone; the green is gone; the lush growth of the hollows above has dwindled here to a grey slime of heat-loving weed that films every wet surface and squelches underfoot. It is the Gloaming with its beautiful mask pulled off — the same warmth, the same wet, but blind and close and scalding, a hothouse with the lamps put out. The line climbs back up toward the far green light; the stair runs down south into a roar of moving water, and east a slick ledge leads off toward a reek of dead timber.

d8.scald.race.name: The Boiling Race
d8.scald.race.description: South the stair drops beside a slot in the rock where all the warm water of the Gloaming above gathers at last and goes mad — a race of near-boiling water roaring down a throat of scoured stone, throwing up a scald of steam that blisters bare skin at the wrong distance and hides the far wall outright. The weed cannot hold in the current; only a crust of grey mineral salt cakes the dry ledges, and a careless foot on the slick between them means the race, and the race means cooking. The flat pale scorpions of the hot pools run this rim too, out for the basking and the wounded both. The stair climbs back north; the ledge runs east along the water into a wider steaming dark.

d8.scald.basin.name: The Simmering Basin
d8.scald.basin.description: The race spills out into a wide floor of terraced pools where the water slows and simmers rather than boils — a hundred steaming basins stepped down the rock, breathing white steam up into a dark so thick with it that the pools show only as a faint scald-glow through the murk. It is the hot, blind heart of this floor. Blistered ferns and grey weed ring the cooler edges; something long-legged and patient shifts once in the steam and goes still, waiting for the warmth of you to come blundering close in the blindness. The race lies back west; south, past the last of the pools, a warmer light leaks up through the steam — steady, and blue, and colder than any fire.

d8.scald.gallery.name: The Blistered Gallery
d8.scald.gallery.description: East off the stair the floor lifts into a long dry gallery the steam does not reach, and it is a graveyard of the green above. Whole mushroom-trunks that came down toward the warmth stand here dead and blistered, their timber split and salt-caked, and the fat ferns that followed them have cooked to brown paper on the stone. Nothing grows; everything grew, once, and reached too far down and died of it. But the old carnivorous blooms found the dead wood good rooting, and more than one blistered stump among the trunks is not a stump at all, only patient, and still bearing its one overripe fruit down here where nothing else is sweet. The stair lies back west; the gallery runs on south, and the air ahead shivers with a dry, rising heat.

d8.scald.vein.name: The Glutton's Seam
d8.scald.vein.description: South of the basin the steam thins and the dark opens into the one lit place on this drowned floor, and it is lovely, and it is the worst place here. A rich seam of raw glimmer threads the whole back wall — a fortune in cold blue-white light caught in the stone, the richest lode a prospector could dream of finding this deep — and the warmth of the pools and the light of the seam together have made this a garden again where nothing else down here can grow. Ferns stand lush and unblistered; overripe emberfruit hangs heavy and glowing in the blue light; and rooted into the very seam, drinking the light and the heat both, grown huge and old and patient on this double bounty, something waits at the heart of the green that all the lesser blooms above are only the children of. The basin lies back north. There is no other way out, and the seam is not going anywhere, and neither is what grows on it.

d8.scald.fissure.name: The Redward Fissure
d8.scald.fissure.description: The gallery ends where the heat wins outright. Here the wet gives up entirely and the rock itself begins to glow — a dull sullen red banked deep in the stone, the first honest fire in a world of borrowed and stolen light — and a fissure splits the floor and falls away steeply downward, breathing up a scorching wind and a low, distant roar, and a light too red and too restless to be anything but the Abyss's own molten blood pooled somewhere below. This is the floor of the Gloaming, and past it the green has no more business: what rises through this crack is the heat that warmed the whole hothouse from underneath, welling up out of the deeper dark. A line has been rigged down the fissure at last, bolted to the cooling rock at the lip and swaying out over the drop in the updraught, and it goes down into the red. The gallery climbs back north.

exitMessages.down: You take the rigged line and go down into the fissure, and the heat comes up to meet you — the scorching updraught tearing at your clothes, the red glow swelling from a banked ember to an open glare, the roar climbing until it is the whole world — and the line pays out and pays out and finally ends on a shelf of scorched black rock in a light that needs no lamp: the floor of the deep at last, where the Abyss bleeds fire.

d9.ember.landing.name: The Cindershelf
d9.ember.landing.description: The rigged line ends on a broad shelf of scorched black rock, and for the first time in all the deep there is no dark to guard against: the whole floor is lit from below and beyond by a restless orange glow that wells up out of the stone instead of being carried down and hoarded against the Tide. The heat sits on everything like a laid hand. Cinders lift off unseen fires and drift glowing across the shelf before they wink out; the rock ticks and groans as it cools and swells; every breath is dry and tastes of hot iron. The rigged line sways back up into the fissure overhead. The shelf runs on south toward a thread of moving light, and east it breaks down into a scatter of hot basins where something lies basking.

exitMessages.up: You catch the rigged line and haul yourself back up into the fissure, the heat falling away hand over hand, the roar dropping to a mutter and then to nothing, until the wet grey stone of the gallery-mouth opens overhead and you climb out onto the floor of the Gloaming.

d9.ember.firethread.name: The Firethread
d9.ember.firethread.description: South of the shelf a stream of molten rock crosses the floor — no wider than a man is tall, but moving, a slow bright ribbon of orange threading a black channel it has cut for itself, skinning over grey and cracking open bright and skinning over again. The heat off it is a wall you lean into to pass. A margin of cooled crust runs alongside where the footing holds, littered with the glassy spatter the thread throws when it gulps, and the air above the flow shivers and swims so that the far wall seems to breathe. The shelf lies back north; eastward, past the head of the thread, a hollow booms and hisses on some slow clock of its own.

d9.ember.geyser.name: The Clockwork Geyser
d9.ember.geyser.description: The thread feeds a deep throat in the floor, and the throat keeps time. For long minutes it only mutters and steams, a black gullet welling slow with molten rock; then it draws a breath the whole room feels, and spits — a roaring column of fire-lit steam and glowing spatter flung to the roof, so bright and so sudden it prints itself on the eye — and falls back, and mutters, and begins again on the same patient count. Prospectors who have watched it swear it keeps the Abyss's own hours, quickening when the Tide runs high. The scalded rock all around is crusted white and gold with the salts the geyser leaves as it breathes. The molten thread runs back west; a low crawl north lifts toward warm basking-stone.

effects.message: The geyser draws the breath you feel in your chest and spits — a roaring column of fire-lit steam and molten spatter erupts over the whole hollow, and there is nowhere on the scalded floor to stand out of it.
effects.roomMessage: The geyser erupts, flinging a roaring column of steam and glowing spatter to the roof.

d9.ember.pools.name: The Basking Pools
d9.ember.pools.description: Here the floor dips into a scatter of shallow basins, and each one holds its own small pool of molten stone — pockets of live orange caught in the black, steaming and healing over and cracking bright again — so the whole hollow is a hearth-field of low lights. Between them the stone is hot enough to bask on and dry enough to hold, and the animals of the deep floor know it: fat newts sprawl belly-down on the warm shelves soaking up a heat that never fails and never dims, and heavier things lie coiled at the pools' edges with their coals showing through the joints of their hide. It is almost peaceful, in the way the garden above was almost peaceful — a warm lit place in the dark that wants you to stay a while. The shelf lies back west, the geyser booms to the south, and a nest of hot cracks opens east.

d9.ember.warren.name: The Cinder Warren
d9.ember.warren.description: East of the pools the floor breaks into a maze of hot cracks and low hollows, a warren of scorched stone webbed with thin bright seams where the fire runs close beneath. This is where the salamanders of the deep floor den: the cracks are worn smooth by their coming and going, littered with shed skins gone to grey ash and the picked, heat-cracked bones of whatever last wandered in warm-blooded, and more than one shadow in the glow is a heavy body waiting out the hottest hour with its eyes half-lidded. They do not fear the light — they are made of it — and they do not care to be crowded in their own dens. The pools lie back west; the warren drops away south, and the glow that way is no longer banked in the stone but open, and enormous.

d9.ember.lake.name: The Molten Heart
d9.ember.lake.description: The warren opens, and the deep ends here, at the thing that has been warming the whole world stacked above it. A lake of molten rock fills the vault wall to wall — no small pool but a slow sea of it, its black skin drifting and cracking into rivers of orange and mending as you watch, throwing up a light so steady and so red that no lamp has ever been needed on all this floor and never will be. The heat is a physical weight against the chest; the air roars softly off the surface; and out over the middle of it drift the ember-motes, sparks of the living fire gathered into slow, turning, watchful knots that belong to the lake and resent anything that comes to cool it. This is the floor of the deep — the heat-vein's own heart, the fire the Abyss bleeds up to feed its green — and there is no down from here, only the lake, and the light, and the long climb back. The warren lies back north.

d7.croft.garden.name: The Kept Garden
d7.croft.garden.description: South of the bower the wild growth stops as if it had struck a wall, and something astonishing takes its place: order. Someone has cut the choke back to bare rock and made a garden of the cleared ground — emberfruit fronds trained up on driven stakes in straight lit rows, beds of steam-fern kept weeded and even, a stand of mushroom-trunks pruned to grow true, a bed of pale caps under a hung lamp. Every fat, too-fleshy bloom that might have turned to teeth has been found and cut out; what grows here grows tame, and the air is sweet with fruit and turned soil instead of sap and rot. A worn path runs on south toward woodsmoke and the low sound of a man talking to himself. The bower and its wild fronds lie back north.

d7.croft.hearth.name: Halloran's Hearth
d7.croft.hearth.description: The path opens into a dry, high nook in the rock that some prospector has made a home of and settled into for good. A cooking fire burns low in a ring of blackened stones, a catch-basin of clean warm seep-water stands beside it, and a pallet of dried fern lies pushed against the far wall under a roof of fitted stone that no canopy can gutter out. Bundles of drying specimens hang from every jut and cranny — pressed fronds, strings of shell, jarred oddities — and it is warm, and lamplit, and safe in a way nowhere else this deep is. The garden lies back north; a low opening east leads to a reek of chemicals and lamp-oil, and west a plank door lets onto the animal-warmth and shuffle of the pens.

d7.croft.study.name: Halloran's Study
d7.croft.study.description: East of the hearth a smaller cell has been given over wholly to the old man's work. A makeshift bench crowds one wall — chipped glass, a clay burner, a mortar worn pale — and every other surface is shelved and stacked with the fruits of a few years' hard looking: a snapper-bloom coiled pale and dead in cloudy fluid, trays of pinned glass-winged insects, a whole wall papered with careful sketches that follow one honest fruiting frond, panel by patient panel, into the thorned and hungry thing it becomes. Nobody down here made this but a man with nothing to do but understand where he'd washed up. A bolted hatch is set into the floor, warm to the touch and breathing heat through its seams. The hearth lies back west.

exitMessages.down: You lower yourself through the hatch onto cold iron rungs and begin to climb down. The shaft goes on, and on — past seams of dead coral and old water, past dark that no lamp of the croft ever reached — and the air thickens and warms with every rung, until at last the rungs end and you step off onto scorched rock in a red, waiting glow, four long depths beneath the garden.

d7.croft.pens.name: The Stonebug Pens
d7.croft.pens.description: West of the hearth, low pens partitioned off with fitted stone hold a small, patient herd of stonebugs, grazing at a bank of glowing moss the old man crops and feeds them by the handful. They are stock, not wild things — grown slow and fat and unafraid in the warm lamplight, kept for the meat and the shell they give, and an old elder-bug sits among them at the head of its kind, plates gone mineral-dark with age, keeping its slow watch over the brood. It smells of moss and chitin and animal warmth, and the shuffle and grind of the herd never quite stops. The hearth lies back east.

d7.throat.name: Where the Dark Goes Bad
d7.throat.description: The low passage south of the camp pinches down to a crawl and then lets go all at once into a colder, larger dark. The roar of the lake is gone; there is no glow here, no moss, no growing thing — the stone is dry and dead and the air sits heavy and still, the way air sits in a place that has not been disturbed in a long time. This is where the prospectors' chalk-line ends and their nerve ended with it: the last stake is hammered into the rock just inside the mouth, the line knotted off and cut, going no further. Beyond it the floor is fouled with the litter of old feeding — cracked shell, gnawed bone, pale moulted husks shed by things that grew too big for them — and something low and wet shifts in the black just past the edge of your light. The camp is back to the north. South and down, the warren opens out.

effects.message: The dark presses close and drinks the warmth from you.

d7.hall.name: The Crawling Hall
d7.hall.description: A vast black gallery, so wide your light cannot find its walls and so high the ceiling is only a guess — a held breath of a cavern, and you the one small warmth in all of it. The floor is a slow tide of pale bodies: gloom-crawlers in their dozens, flowing over one another and the heaped middens of their feeding, a dry endless rasp of flesh on stone that comes from every side at once. They do not mind the dark; they own it. Raise a light and the nearest of them rouse and turn toward the warmth of it, the whole tide stirring your way — and somewhere out in the black the larger shapes shift, the old ones, too big to hurry and too patient to need to. A side passage breaks off west into a narrower dark; eastward the floor falls away into a reek of old bone; the warren runs on south, deeper; the throat climbs back north.

d7.drift.name: The Moulting Drift
d7.drift.description: The west passage opens into a long, low drift where the crawlers come to shed. The floor climbs in soft pale dunes of moulted husk, knee-deep in places, dry and brittle and whispering underfoot — years of it, the cast skins of a colony that never leaves. The air is thick with a chalk-fine dust that catches in the throat. Bigger husks lie further in, split lengthwise and big as a man, the moults of the old ones; and where the dunes bank against the far wall a seam of raw glimmer crusts the stone, lit cold and faint by its own deep glow, never yet worked by any hand. The hall lies back east.

d7.midden.name: The Bone Field
d7.midden.description: East of the hall the floor drops a few feet into a wide, low chamber that the warren uses to discard what it cannot eat. The ground is a field of bone — cracked, gnawed, picked clean and pale, heaped in slow drifts against the walls and crunching to splinters underfoot — the leavings of a colony that has fed here a very long time. Rat-bone and bug-shell and the long bones of bigger things lie tangled together, and here and there among them the unmistakable shape of a delver: a staved-in helm, a boot with the foot still in it, a ribcage swept clean. Scour-slugs haul themselves over the heaps in the dark, working the last of the marrow, leaving their clean wet tracks across the dead. Somewhere under the drifts, the crawlers' middens have buried whatever the dead carried down. The hall is back west, up the lip.

d7.gallery.name: The Long Gallery
d7.gallery.description: The second great cavern of the warren, longer than it is wide — a black gallery that runs on south past the reach of any light a delver carries, the floor sloping gently down the whole way as though the stone itself is leaning toward the deep. A seam of rust-red iron ore shows in one wall, worth the working for a delver with the arm to spare. The crawlers are thinner here than in the hall but not gone; they flow along the walls and the long centre in loose, restless streams, all of them moving the same way, downslope, as though something below were calling the whole tide of them down. A side gallery opens west, and from it comes a dry, massed rasping louder than anywhere else in the warren. At the gallery's low end the floor gives out entirely into a black shaft dropping down; the hall lies back north.

d7.brood.name: The Brood-Heart
d7.brood.description: The west gallery chokes down and then opens into a chamber where the crawlers are not streams but a single heaving mass — they pile here thicker than anywhere else in the warren, layered over one another wall to wall and floor to unseen ceiling, a slow churning carpet of pale flesh that never quite stills and never quite quiets, the dry rasp of them filling the dark like surf. Among the smaller bodies the old ones lie half-buried and content, vast and unhurried, and the cast husks drift waist-deep in the corners. Where the far wall shows through the press of them, a vein of raw glimmer threads the rock in dull blue-white seams — a rich find, in a place that means to kill you for the reaching of it. Whatever this place is to them — a heart, a hold, the centre they all return to — they suffer no light in it gladly, and the nearest of them are already turning your way. The only way back is east, the way you came.

d7.chasm.name: The Black Chimney
d7.chasm.description: The gallery's long slope bottoms out in a rough chimney of wet, fluted rock — a narrow well of dark where the warren shows its true depth at last. This is no cave the prospectors cut or the crawlers wore — it is a natural flue worn by water long gone, and it does not end at the ledge you land on. Below your feet the chimney plunges on, down and down past the reach of any light you can throw, a cold breath rising out of it that is older and emptier than the warren above; no rope has ever been made fast here, no stake driven, and a dropped stone falls a long, long time before any sound comes back. That way lies deeper than anyone has gone. Where the old water polished the flue smooth, a pale vein of silver ore runs through the rock within arm's reach of the ledge. For now there is only the ledge: a low passage breaks off it, leading on into a dead and lightless hush, and the gallery's slope climbs back north. One stretch of the western wall is split by a flowstone-choked fissure, and a faint draught breathes out of it — warmer and wetter than the warren's dead air, carrying the far ghost of running water, as though the crack climbed toward the living river somewhere high above.

exitMessages.up: You haul yourself up the prospectors' rigging, pitch after pitch of smooth-worn flue, the far ghost of the river growing to a roar, and come up dripping beside the plunge-pool.

d7.hush.name: The Hush
d7.hush.description: The passage off the ledge opens into a small, square chamber, and the moment you cross into it the dark changes its quality — the rasp of the crawlers cuts off behind you, the air goes dead and close, and your light shrinks back to a guttering knot, as though the room itself disapproved of it. The walls here are worked: cut square and true, faced smooth by patient hands long ago, and across the far wall an old relief has been carved — a band of flowing Umbral glyphs gone soft at the edges with age, and at their centre a single hard mark, struck deep and unmistakable in any tongue. It means turn back. Whatever the deep-folk knew when they cut it, they cut it to keep something on the far side of this room, and they did not come back to tend it. The crawlers will not cross the worked threshold; they gather at the passage mouth behind you and no further. Something grows here even so: in the cracks of the old worked stone a pale, lightless fungus has taken hold, and a few grubs inch blindly through it, chewing their slow pale trails — the only life willing to share this room with what waits beyond it. Ahead, south, the dark waits. The chimney is back north.

d7.relict.name: The Last Claim
d7.relict.description: Past the warded room the worked stone gives way to raw cave again, and here, against the wall, someone made a last stand. A prospector sits where they died, propped against the rock with a spent lantern in their lap and a pick laid across their knees, gone to leather and bone in the dry cold but otherwise undisturbed — even the scour-slugs do not come this far down. They got further than anyone: past the swarm, past the warded hush, almost to the bottom of the warren. On the wall beside them, scratched in chalk by a failing hand, a last claim is staked over the seam it marks — a name, a date, and above them a vein of raw glimmer that threads the rock in dull blue-white, the strike they came all this way for. Beneath the claim, smaller: *it drinks the light. do not bring a light.* A few shards have spilled from a rotted pouch, and in their cupped hands, held to the end, is a single crystal they had worked loose before the dark found them. South, the cave opens into a deeper black; north leads back to the hush.

d7.lair.name: The Drowned Black
d7.lair.description: The cave opens south into the deepest hollow of the warren, and the dark here is wrong. It is not merely the absence of light but the appetite for it: your flame shrinks and gutters the moment you arrive, the glow pulled thin as though something close by were drinking it, and the cold leans in against your skin like a held breath. The crawlers do not follow you in — even they keep to the edges of this place, gathered at the rim of your light and no closer, watching the centre with their blind faces turned. For there is a centre. A deeper black coils in the heart of the cave, shifting against the dark in a way that has nothing to do with your light, and as you understand what you are looking at, it understands that you have come. This is where the prospectors' road ends, and the dead one's chalk was right. But the warren does not end here. Where the coiling dark has kept its long vigil, the floor of the hollow gives way to a black throat going further down — a drowned shaft past where any prospector ever cut a step. The way back is north; the dark goes down.

d8.drop.name: The Sounding Drop
d8.drop.description: The throat in the floor of the Drowned Black drops you into a wet, sounding dark — a shaft worn round and smooth by water that has not run here in a long age, the rope you fixed above creaking somewhere out of sight. You come down onto a slick shelf of black stone, and the air changes: colder, heavier, thick with the smell of standing water and old rot. Somewhere below and ahead a steady drip counts time, and under it, faint and constant, a deeper roar you feel in the rock more than hear. This is past where any prospector ever cut a step — the warren keeps going down, and so, it seems, did something else. The shaft climbs up the way you came; the dark opens south.

d8.cistern.name: The Cistern
d8.cistern.description: The shaft opens into a broad, low chamber that once held water and mostly still does — a black sheet of it lies still across the floor, ankle-deep and colder than the air, and your steps send slow rings out to walls you cannot see. Worked stone shows here and there under the silt, the same patient Umbral cutting found higher up, drowned and forgotten. Passages run off in every direction, their mouths all alike in the dark, and the still water carries every sound and gives back nothing of where it came from. It would be very easy to lose the way you came in. The drip and the distant roar come from the east; other ways run west and south, and the drop climbs north.

d8.plungefoot.name: The Plunge Foot
d8.plungefoot.description: East the floor tilts down and the roar resolves into what makes it: a hard, cold column of water falling out of a crack in the high dark and hammering into a churning black pool, throwing up a freezing spray that fills the whole chamber. The cold of it drowns your flame the moment you step in, a hiss of steam and then nothing, and the dark closes whole. This is the foot of some great fall — the overspill of the drowned country far above come down at last to the bottom of the world. The water deafens. A wet ledge climbs north along the cataract; a low passage runs back west, and another east.

effects.message: The freezing spray off the plunge drowns your flame in a gout of steam.

d8.ledge.name: The Chasm Ledge
d8.ledge.description: The ledge climbs along the falling water onto a lip of wet stone above a chasm so deep your light, if you had any, would not find its bottom — and here the spray puts your flame out and holds it out, so you stand in the roaring dark. But the dark is not whole. Far, far up the shaft, past more fallen floors than you care to count, a single thread of pale water catches a light you cannot reach: a faint blue-white glimmer of moss-glow on spray, high and cold and impossibly distant. You would swear it for the black lake spilling over the Far Bank, the same water you walked beside an age of descent ago — fallen all this way down the throat of the world to break apart somewhere in the dark above your head. And the ledge does not dead-end after all: bolted into the wet lip of it, a long hanging bridge of glimmer-wire and black Umbral plank runs out north across the chasm, swaying under the fall's cold breath until the dark takes it. The way back drops south down the ledge; the bridge goes north, out over nothing.

effects.message: Cold blown spray off the chasm snuffs your flame, and the roaring dark closes in.

d8.span.name: The Span
d8.span.description: Out on the bridge the world falls away on every side. The planks sway underfoot on their glimmer-wire, slick with the fall's spray, and the great column of water comes down close on your right hand — a hard, cold pillar out of the high dark, thundering past into a drop your light will never sound. There is nothing here but the roar and the sway and the vast wet space of the shaft all around you, and far, far overhead that one thread of blue-white moss-glow on the falling water, cold and impossibly high. Halfway across, and the dark on the far side is not like the dark behind: it is thicker, and it waits. The near end of the bridge climbs back south to the ledge; the planks run on north.

effects.message: Cold spray off the falling water drowns your flame, and the roaring dark closes in.

d8.wardpost.name: The Ward-Post
d8.wardpost.description: The bridge lands at last on a shelf of worked stone, and here — out over the abyss, at the very end of the deep-folk's reach — someone set a ward and never took it down. A cold glimmer-lamp burns in a carved cradle, blue-white and steady, and around it the old hand has scored the warding-lines and pinned them with stakes of raw glimmer, all of it faced north into the dark. It is the same light that fills the sanctuary far above, set here to the same purpose: against the dark, and the things the dark makes. Within the ring the air is dry and still, and you may stand in your own shadow again. Beyond it the ground rises north into close-set stone and a colder black, and the wards lean that way, holding, against whatever keeps to the graves. The bridge sways back south over the chasm; north, the warded light gives out.

d8.necropolis.gate.name: The Grave-Gate
d8.necropolis.gate.description: Past the ward-post's light the rising ground levels into a threshold of worked stone, and here the deep-folk set their last edge against this place — a second warding cut into the sill, twin to the one that still burns behind you. This one is dead. The glimmer-stakes are drawn or snuffed, the chalked lines scuffed to grey, and the dark has long since come back over it and settled in to stay. Beyond the dead line the air changes: colder, older, dry as a shut room, and heavy with the sense of a great many things kept close and gone quiet. Rows of low grave-niches begin in the walls to either hand, most of them gaping empty. Something shuffles in the black ahead, slow and without hurry, and does not much mind your coming. The warded light lies back south; north the ground opens into the graves.

d8.necropolis.causeway.name: The Bone Causeway
d8.necropolis.causeway.description: The floor narrows to a raised bank of fitted stone that runs on north between two sunken fields of the dead — a causeway lifted above the graves so the living once need not walk among them. That care is long spent. To either side, below the lip of the bank, the dark lies deeper than dark, and out of it the standing husks come, hauling themselves up the low walls in slow, patient numbers, drawn to the one warmth crossing their ground. The dead here are the old dead, drawn up whole and heavy from the deep niches, and they do not scatter and do not stop. The gate lies back south; the causeway runs north toward a wider black, and a low seam breaks off west into the niche-walls.

d8.necropolis.niches.name: The Niche-Walls
d8.necropolis.niches.description: The west seam opens into a dead-end vault where the grave-niches are cut deepest and thickest — the walls honeycombed floor to ceiling with them, tier on tier of shallow dark mouths, the whole chamber a wall of graves closing overhead. It is the stillest place in the necropolis and the worst. The wrapped dead lie undisturbed here, folded in their niches as they were laid, and among them, folded just the same, are things that are not dead and not still — the dark keeps its hunters where the dead are thickest, wedged back in the niches and pressed flat to the ceiling, unlit and unmoving until your light strays too close. The only way out is back east to the causeway.

d8.necropolis.plaza.name: The Ancestor Plaza
d8.necropolis.plaza.description: The causeway lets out into a wide open ground, the heart of the necropolis — a plaza of worn flagstones ringed with squat stone towers, chullpas raised for the honoured dead to keep watch over their kin from within. Here the space opens out and the ceiling lifts away into a dark your light cannot find, and for a moment there is room to breathe and set your back to something. But the low doors of the chullpas are black open mouths, the flagstones are scattered with the grey offerings the deep-folk once left their dead, and nothing keeps watch from the towers now but the dark itself. The husks drift across the plaza without purpose until they find your warmth. The causeway runs back south; north a walled way leads deeper, toward a low reek of old bone.

d8.necropolis.ossuary.name: The Ossuary
d8.necropolis.ossuary.description: North of the plaza the walls close in and the way becomes a long, low store of bone. When the deep niches filled, the deep-folk laid their oldest dead here in heaps and courses, skull upon skull and long-bone stacked like cordwood, tier on tier into a dark that gives back nothing. The air is thick with the dry, cold reek of it. This is where the dead are densest, and so this is where the most of them stand: bound husks haul themselves out of the courses where they were stacked, and the dark between the heaps holds things that were never bones at all. The plaza lies back south; a walled processional runs on north, straight and deliberate, toward whatever the whole necropolis was built to face.

d8.necropolis.procession.name: The Silent Procession
d8.necropolis.procession.description: The way straightens into a walled processional, a long dark aisle raised for the carrying of the dead — the stone underfoot worn smooth by feet that stopped coming an age ago, the walls to either hand close and blind. There is a purpose to it that the rest of the necropolis lacks: everything here points north, toward the far end of the aisle, where the ground gathers itself and rises to the foot of a great sealed tomb. The dead stand sparse along the procession, as if even the husks keep back from what waits at its head. The ossuary lies back south; ahead the aisle ends at the tomb-foot.

d8.necropolis.vault.name: The Tomb-Foot
d8.necropolis.vault.description: The procession ends at the foot of the greatest tomb of all — a chullpa raised head and shoulders above the rest, set apart at the head of the necropolis and reached by a steep stone ladder that climbs the tower's face to a single low door high in the dark above. This is the threshold the whole place was built around, and it is not unkept. The Aya-Keeper holds it: a deep-folk glimmer-singer the Hollowing all but finished, still moving among the graves with the ghost of an old tending, raising the dead where they fall and turning the gathered dark on anything that would climb to the sealed door. It will not let you pass while it stands. The procession lies back south; the ladder climbs up into the crypt above.

d8.necropolis.crypt.name: The Sealed Crypt
d8.necropolis.crypt.description: The ladder brings you up through the low door into the sealed heart of the tomb — and the dark here is the deep kind, the kind that leans on the skin and drinks a flame and gives nothing back, worse than any dark below it. The deep-folk raised this crypt highest of all and sealed it and warded the whole long way to it: the graves, the procession, the ward-post out over the abyss, all of it set facing this one door. It was never enough. Whatever they interred here drank down the dark of an age of lightless keeping and stood up wearing it, and it has been waiting in the black at the top of the world's oldest grave for the wardings to fail and a warmth to climb the ladder. It knows you are here. The killing cold gathers, and the only way back is down.

effects.message: The killing dark of the crypt leans close and drinks the warmth and the will out of you.
effects.roomMessage: The dark presses in, cold and hungry.

d8.silt.name: Silt Crawl
d8.silt.description: West of the cistern the ceiling comes down and the floor comes up to meet it, until the way is a low silt-floored crawl through drowned stone, the water gone to cold black mud that sucks at hand and knee. Pale things have been through here recently — the mud is churned with the wide, dragging trails the crawlers leave, and the trails are fresh. The smell of them is heavy here, that wet-coal reek of the warren, stronger than anywhere above. The crawl carries on south and opens west into something larger and worse-smelling still; the cistern lies back east.

north.name: a low seam in the north wall, half-curtained by hanging silt

d8.backwash.name: The Backwash
d8.backwash.description: The crawl opens into a stagnant backwash where the standing water has gone foul, skinned over with a grey scum that breaks in slow rafts as something moves beneath it. The walls here are wrong — not crawler-work but the deeper dark itself, threads of that light-drinking black clinging in the seams the way it gathered around the thing in the Drowned Black, thicker the further you press. The crawlers here have taken the taint of it; they reach for your warmth and seem, for a moment, to drink it. A run climbs north back to the crawl; another opens east toward worked steps.

d8.broodsump.name: Broodsump
d8.broodsump.description: The west crawl spills into a vaulted sump that the warren has made wholly its own — the floor heaped with moulted husks and the pale, glistening litter of a nest, the walls furred with egg-strings strung between the worked-stone ribs of a chamber the Umbrals cut for some forgotten use — one rib laid bare where a thick vein of raw glimmer threads the stone, cold and faintly alight beneath the filth, too dim to lift the dark. The reek is overwhelming. And at the heart of it, vast and pallid and swollen past the size of any crawler you have met, something lifts its blind front from the brood and turns it toward the warmth of you. This is what the warren is for. This is what has been feeding it. The only way out is back east, the way you came.

west.name: a flooded squeeze behind the nest the brood drag through, falling away west toward water

d8.cache.name: The Drowned Cache
d8.cache.description: East of the plunge the spray thins and the passage ends in a dry pocket lifted just above the water-line — and someone reached it before you. A prospector's cache sits tucked into a worked niche: an oiled roll gone black with damp, a strongbox forced and emptied long since, and the scatter of what the finders did not think worth carrying back up through all that dark. What they came all this way for is still here, barely touched — a rich vein of raw glimmer threading the back of the niche, cold mineral light caught in the rock. They got this far down and turned around. Whether they made it back up, the cache does not say. The plunge roars west, the only way back — though the back of the niche does not quite ring solid.

east.name: a hairline crack behind the cache, squeezing east into the dark

d8.steps.name: The Weeping Steps
d8.steps.description: South of the cistern the drowned cave gives way, abruptly, to worked stone again — a flight of shallow Umbral steps descending into the dark, each tread dished and weeping a thin film of water that runs down them step to step in a constant, glassy sheet. The cutting is old and fine and utterly unlike the warren's mindless tunnelling; whoever made this meant a road. Where the old chisels bit deepest into the wall beside the steps, the cut has laid bare a pale seam of silver threading the dark stone, unworked. The crawlers do not seem to like it here — their trails stop at the top step. The steps go down south toward more worked stone; the cave runs north back to the cistern and west to the backwash.

d8.reliefs.name: The Waking Reliefs
d8.reliefs.description: The steps end in a long worked gallery, both walls carved end to end with Umbral relief — and you have seen their like before, far above, where the figures of the deep-folk lengthened and starved into something wrong as you followed them down. Here the carving has gone all the way over. The figures on these walls are gaunt past humanity, hands curled to claws, eyes gouged wide, and the last of them are not carved at all but clawed: raw furrows hacked into the stone by something that learned the shape from the inside. A thin seam of cold blue-white glimmer threads the ruined work, and the air carries a faint, sour wrongness, like a clean place gone bad. Something moved here not long ago, and left a thing standing watch. The gallery runs south toward a colder dark; the steps climb back north.

d8.watch.name: The Hollow Watch
d8.watch.description: The gallery opens into a round, domed chamber — a watch-post, or a shrine, the worked stone here finest of all, ringed with relief faces that all turn inward toward a single low dais. The faces are starved and clawed like the rest. On the dais, things have been set out with a terrible, broken care: worked chitin plates laid in a row, a cold-lamp long dead, glyphs scratched over glyphs in patterns that almost mean something and then don't. And tending them, rising now from its work to fix you with eyes gone raw and wide in the dark, is the keeper of this place — tall and grey and stretched wrong, the soft blue-green glow of the deep-folk still moving under its skin, the last unbroken thing about it. It was a glimmer-singer once. It still remembers the shape of the craft, if not its purpose, and it does not mean to let you pass. A black stair descends south, deeper than this; the gallery runs back north.

d8.stair.name: The Umbral Stair
d8.stair.description: Past the watch-post the worked road gathers itself for one last descent: a broad black stair going down into a dark so complete it seems to swallow the very idea of light. The walls to either side are dense with Umbral symbol and relief, but these are different from the failing work above — cut deep, sure, and unbroken, the same patient hand all the way down, marks of warding and welcome both. None of the warren's trails reach here; the gaunt things of the dark will not cross the threshold the deep-folk cut, whether it was meant to hold the dark out or to hold something in. The stair goes down into a kept quiet; the gallery lies back north.

d8.camp.name: The Prospectors' Last Camp
d8.camp.description: Behind the cache the niche narrows to a crack you would never have squeezed through if you had not seen it — and beyond it the rock opens into a dry, still pocket the prospectors kept for their own. Whoever emptied the cache outside holed up here — as far down as their claim ever ran, the last dry ground before the dark below: a cold fire-ring set under a natural flue that draws the smoke away, a catch-basin walled in clay to take the clean seep threading down one wall, a battered oil-lamp left standing on a flat stone. It is untouched, exactly as they left it — bedrolls folded, a pot upturned by the ash — as though they meant to come back. They did not. But the makings are all sound, and the dark does not find its way in here. Strike the lamp, light the fire, drink, and gather yourself before going on. The only way out is back west, through the crack to the cache.

d8.grotto.name: The Slug Grotto
d8.grotto.description: A seam in the silt-crawl wall, easy to crawl past in the dark, opens into a low domed grotto — and after so long blind, the faint glow of it stops you where you stand. Pale mushrooms crowd the damp seams of the wall, each breathing a weak, steady light, and a great fan of witchglow spreads cold and green across the far rock, enough together to see by at last. Grey scour-slugs the length of a forearm haul themselves over the stone among the beds, grazing the fungus and leaving clean tracks behind, blind and untroubled by the light. Someone tried to make this place a camp once: a scatter of spilled gear lies rotting in a corner — a burst pack, a snapped pole, a single boot, a fire never lit — left where it dropped, with no bones to say whether they walked out or only the slugs cleaned up after. The crawl runs back south to the silt.

d9.sanctuary.landing.name: The Sanctuary Threshold
d9.sanctuary.landing.description: The black stair bottoms out, and for the first time in a long descent the dark draws back of its own accord. A cold blue-green glimmer-lamp burns in a niche of worked stone — kept, tended, alive — throwing a soft, heatless light over a low threshold chamber swept clean and laid with care. After the drowned warren and the clawed reliefs above, the quiet here is almost a sound of its own, and nothing of the dark you climbed through has followed you down past the warded stair. This is the edge of something the deep-folk still hold whole: a sanctuary cut into the deep rock, warded against the dark and the things the dark makes. An inner way leads deeper south, sealed for now behind a worked door that does not yet open. The stair climbs back north into the dark you came from.

d9.sanctuary.hall.name: The Warded Hall
d9.sanctuary.hall.description: South of the threshold the worked stone opens into a long hall, and here the warding the deep-folk cut is plain to see. Cold glimmer-lamps burn at intervals down its length, blue-white and heatless, and the relief between them is the old, sure hand — deep marks of warding and welcome running unbroken to either side, none of them clawed, none of them failing. The air is dry and still and clean of the wet-coal reek you climbed through; the dark does not come in here, and the gaunt things of the dark do not cross the cut. It is the first place in a long descent that was made to keep something safe rather than to keep something out. The threshold climbs back north; the hall opens south into a soft glow.

d9.sanctuary.shrine.name: The Umbral Shrine
d9.sanctuary.shrine.description: The hall opens into a round, vaulted shrine at the heart of the kept place — and the light here is its own, a great knot of raw glimmer cradled in worked stone, breathing a steady blue-white glow that fills the chamber and pains nothing. This is the source of the warding you have walked through: a light the deep-folk set against the dark and the things the dark makes, and it has not gone out. The walls are carved end to end with the old hand — the deep-folk gathered and bowed at the foot of the glow — and a shallow font below the relief catches a clean thread of water seeping through the rock. Nothing of the warren reaches here. The dark you climbed through stops at the cut, and for the first time since the Rim you may set down your guard, drink, and gather yourself. Ways open east to a soft green glow and west toward still water; the hall climbs back north. And from the heart of the shrine, behind the glimmer-cradle, a broad black stair goes down — long and worn and sure, the old hand's work running on into the deep below the kept place. Far down its throat the dark is not whole: a faint living glow rises to meet you, and on the slow air comes the green smell of growing things and tended water. Whatever the deep-folk keep down there, they keep it still — and the stair goes down to it.

d9.sanctuary.garden.name: The Glow Garden
d9.sanctuary.garden.description: East of the shrine the chamber widens into a tended garden of light — bed upon bed of pale mushrooms breathing their weak, steady glow, and a great fan of witchglow spread cold and green across the far wall, all of it crowding the warded light the way fungus crowds any glow in the deep. It has the look of something once kept and now only left to grow: old planting-rows gone soft at the edges, the deep-folk's work carried on by nothing but the dark's own slow gardening. Armoured grey stonebugs trundle the beds on patient mandibles, and squat thornbugs bristle between the fattest caps, none of them troubled by you — there is forage enough here, and the light keeps the hunters out. The shrine lies back west.

d9.sanctuary.hollow.name: The Grub Hollow
d9.sanctuary.hollow.description: The cistern's rim slopes down into a damp, low hollow where the glow runs thin and the fungus grows soft and rotten-sweet. Pale grubs chew slow trails through the rot, and grey scour-slugs haul themselves over the stone leaving clean tracks behind, grazing whatever the sanctuary sheds, carried down with the water — the quiet cleaners at the bottom of the lit food web, doing here in the warded calm what they do everywhere there is light to feed under. A stonebug or two grinds among them, unhurried. There is nothing here that means you harm. Still water lies back north up the slope, and a darker seam opens south where the warded light at last gives out.

d9.sanctuary.cistern.name: The Kept Cistern
d9.sanctuary.cistern.description: West of the shrine a still pool lies in a basin of worked stone, kept apart and kept clean — not the drowned black of the warren above but clear, cold water, walled round with the patient Umbral cutting and lit blue-white by a glimmer-lamp set in the rock above it. This is the deep-folk's own water, held separate from the great fall below; pale blind cave-fish drift the surface in slow, blind circles, and moss and lit fungus crowd the damp rim where a stonebug grazes the banks. Nothing has fouled it. The shrine lies east, the rim slopes south down toward the grub hollow, and at the pool's far end worked steps drop away toward a colder roar — the great fall, somewhere below.

d10.sanctuary.basin.name: The Plunge Basin
d10.sanctuary.basin.description: The stair from the kept cistern descends past the edge of the warded light into a wide, dark cavern loud with falling water — and as your eyes give up, the dark is not quite whole, for high overhead a hard, cold column of water comes down out of the black and hammers into a churning pool, and the spray of it carries a faint blue-white glow. This is the foot of the great fall: the same black water you watched break apart far above you from the chasm ledge, the Far Bank's lake come all the way down the throat of the world to land here at the bottom. Curtains of weeping chasm-moss hang from the wet rock above the pool, trailing their hair-fine tendrils into the spray and glowing cold in the dark, and where the water has scoured the rock face a thin seam of raw glimmer threads the broken stone. The shrine's light does not reach here, and the things that shun it have crept to the water's edge — pale blind salamanders basking in the damp, an eyeless tremor-mole reading the stone for footfall. The worn stair climbs back up toward the kept water and its glow.

d9.sanctuary.run.name: The Centipede Run
d9.sanctuary.run.description: South of the hollow the warded light fails altogether and the worked stone gives way to a raw, dim run in the natural rock — the margin of the kept place, where the deep-folk's cutting stops and the dark begins again. The shrine's light does not reach this far, and so the hunters do: the dim-edge things that work wherever prey strays out of the glow. Whip-fast lengths of armoured segment pour over the rock on a hundred legs, antennae testing the air, fond of exactly this kind of border between the safe and the dark. They are the price of the edge. The hollow and its glow lie back north; the run climbs away up into a cramped seam, and out of it drifts a thin, sour reek — a clean place gone bad, the smell of rot worked over and over by something patient in the dark.

d8.midden.seam.name: The Sour Seam
d8.midden.seam.description: The unmapped dark at the foot of the Centipede Run is not, it turns out, unending — the seam doubles back and climbs, worming up through old sealed diggings into a pocket the deep-folk closed off and the warden's light never cleaned. The reek thickens as you go: a sour, eating stink, rot turned over and over until it has gone past rot into something that stings the eyes. The walls are scored everywhere with pale, smoking tracks where something caustic has hauled itself through, and grey scour-slugs graze the slime in the dark, blind and unbothered. Whatever this place was, the deep-folk gave their dead to it. The seam drops back down toward the run; it opens south into a wider, fouler dark.

d8.midden.pit.name: The Reeking Midden
d8.midden.pit.description: The seam opens into the midden proper — a long, low charnel where the kept place above let down its dead, ages of them, heaped and gnawed and given over wholesale to the cleaners. Bones lie drifted to the walls, picked glass-clean and stacked by no hand but the slow industry of slugs, and the floor between is a living carpet of them: grey scour-slugs in their dozens, hauling over the heaps and over each other, grazing the dead in blind, untroubled multitudes. It is not the slugs that turn the stomach but the smell — sour past bearing, the reek of a thing that eats and eats and never cleans anymore. The seam climbs back north; the midden runs on south, where the slime pools deeper.

d8.midden.gorge.name: The Gorge Pools
d8.midden.gorge.description: Here the floor dips and the slime no longer smears but pools — shallow, fuming basins of the slugs' sour leavings, eating slow bowls into the very stone and breathing a haze that pricks the eyes and throat. This is where the fat ones gather: slugs swollen monstrous on the endless dead, too gorged and too changed to graze quietly anymore. They turn their blind, dripping fronts toward your warmth and begin, ponderously, to come. A faint, clean glow leaks from a cleft in the west wall, out of place in all this rot. The midden lies back north; the floor falls away south into a deeper dark.

effects.message: The caustic pools fume around your feet, and the sour slime eats at you where it splashes.

d8.midden.alcove.name: The Witchglow Cleft
d8.midden.alcove.description: The cleft in the west wall opens into a dry, lifted pocket the slime never reached — and after the fuming dark the cold green glow of it is a mercy. A great fan of witchglow spreads across the back rock, breathing its steady light, and pale mushrooms crowd the seams below it; the air here is only damp and clean, the reek shut out behind you. Someone sheltered here once, where the slugs could not climb and the sour pools could not reach: a scatter of left gear lies dry in a corner, waiting for a delver who never came back up the seam. The only way on is back east, into the gorge.

d8.midden.descent.name: The Sour Descent
d8.midden.descent.description: South of the pools the floor tilts and drops, a slime-glazed chute of stone slick enough to take your feet from under you, all of it running with the slow downhill seep of the gorge above. The reek is a physical thing now, thick enough to taste, and the smoking slugtracks all run the same way — down, toward whatever lies at the bottom of the midden that everything else feeds. A few gorged slugs work the slick, and the dark below them is not empty: something very large shifts in it, and the whole chute trembles. The pools lie back north; the descent bottoms out south.

d8.midden.lair.name: The Glut's Pit
d8.midden.lair.description: The descent bottoms out in a great round pit, the deepest cut of the midden, and the floor of it is a single seething flood of caustic slime that hisses and smokes against the stone. Everything the warren and the kept place ever let fall came down at last to here — and so did the thing that ate it. It fills the pit wall to wall, a hill of grey glistening flesh swollen past any natural size, blind and certain and already turning toward the warmth of you. There is nothing kept here anymore, nothing cleaned; only the Great Glut, and the long dark it has been growing in. Where the deep's old fires once licked the pit wall a band of dark, glassy fulgurite runs fused through the stone, throwing back what light you carry in a dull, oily sheen — but you will not get near it while the Glut still holds the pit. The only way out is back north, up the chute.

effects.message: The flood of caustic slime laps at you, hissing, and burns wherever it touches.
effects.roomMessage: The seething slime hisses and smokes.

d10.fields.stair.name: The Field Stair
d10.fields.stair.description: The long stair from the shrine bottoms out at last, and the deep you dreaded opens instead onto a low, lit cavern that someone has been keeping for a very long time. The faint glow you followed down the throat of the stair is the work of a hundred drifting lightbugs and bed upon bed of tended fungus, and the air is not the dead air of the warren but a living one — damp, green, faintly sweet, moving with the breath of running water somewhere south. Worked stone underfoot gives way to soft, dark earth gone to crop; the old hand has carried on down here, but down here it still tends rather than wards. Nothing means you harm. The stair climbs back north toward the shrine's glow; south the cavern opens wide into a lit common of fields.

d10.fields.commons.name: The Lightfield Commons
d10.fields.commons.description: The fields open out into a broad lit common, and for the first time on the long way down the deep looks less like a wound in the world than a place lived in. Tended rows of luminous fungus run the floor in even crops, breathing a soft blue-green light; lightbugs drift thick overhead in a slow, shifting constellation, and the glow of them moves wherever they wander. Armoured grey stonebugs graze the rows on patient mandibles and bristling thornbugs trundle between the fattest caps, none of them troubled by you — there is forage enough, and no hunter has ever been let down this far. This is the Umbrals' own ground, kept and cropped and quiet. The stair climbs back north; moss terraces lie west, a worked water-channel runs east, and south the rows run on toward the close-set roofs and lamplit cuts of a village — but the way there drops through a low warded gate that stands shut for now, and does not yet open to you.

d10.fields.terraces.name: The Moss Terraces
d10.fields.terraces.description: West of the common the floor climbs in worked terraces against the cavern wall, each step furred with a tended, even crop of luminous fungus and hung with long curtains of weeping chasm-moss that the deep-folk train down the wet stone and crop for its silk. The light here is soft and damp and green, and the rot at the foot of the terraces is rich: pale grubs chew slow trails through it, the quiet cleaners doing here in the kept calm what they do under any glow. A stonebug grinds among them, unhurried, and the lightbugs drift in over the steps and out again. The common lies back east.

d10.fields.channel.name: The Tended Channel
d10.fields.channel.description: East of the common a clear stream wells up cold from a cleft in the rock and runs in a worked channel cut straight and true through the lit ground — the deep-folk's water, led where they want it to feed the beds. It is not the drowned black of the warren but living water, breathing a faint glow off the fungus crowding its banks, and lightbugs come down to drift the surface and lift away again. The channel runs south, gathering, toward the broad shine of a kept lake. The common lies back west.

d10.fields.lake.name: The Keeper's Lake
d10.fields.lake.description: The channel opens into a broad, still lake held in a basin of worked stone, lit blue-green by the crop crowding its banks and by the lightbugs that drift low over the water. This is the kept lake, the bottom of the deep-folk's water and the richest of it: pale blind cave-fish hang thick in the cold shallows, and a patient hand with a baited line — the grubs the terraces breed are the surest bait — could take a good few. At the lake's far end the water gathers, narrows, and slides away into a black cleft in the cavern wall, gone down into the rock to wherever the deep takes it, and does not come back. East, across a worked stone causeway the deep-folk never built, a scatter of surface lamplight burns where no Umbral light should be — the pickets of a prospectors' camp, the first human ground this deep. The channel runs back north toward the common.

d10.camp.picket.name: The Lastlight Picket
d10.camp.picket.description: The causeway lets you off the kept water onto ground no Umbral ever worked — a picket of driven stakes, oil-lamps on poles, and a hand-painted board reading LASTLIGHT nailed crooked to the rock. This is the first human foothold this deep: the far end of a road that started at the Rim, ten floors up, and the few who walked the whole of it holed up here where the deep-folk's fields end and the true dark begins. The lamps burn surface-yellow against the fields' cold blue-green glow across the water, a stubborn little warmth in a place that was never meant for it. A worked iron seam runs the near wall, half-dug, a pick left leaning in it. The causeway runs back west to the lake; east, past the stakes, the camp proper opens into a lamplit common.

d10.camp.commons.name: The Lastlight Common
d10.camp.commons.description: The camp opens into a low, lamplit common dug back under an overhang and fenced from the dark with driven stakes and a ring of oil-lamps kept always burning. A fire-pit crackles at its heart — real flame, hauled down as coals and fed on dried moss, the one open fire this deep — and around it stand the plank bunks, gear-lines, and upturned crates of folk who came down with the Rush and, against every odd, made the whole descent and stayed. Kit is stacked ready for the drop below: coils of line, patched packs, spare lamps trimmed and filled. This is the heart of Lastlight, and its captain holds court by the fire. The picket lies west on the lake; a canvas-walled bench sits south where the reek of reagents leaks out; east the ground runs to the rope-head and the dark beyond.

d10.camp.bench.name: Corvane's Bench
d10.camp.bench.description: A canvas lean-to walled off south of the common, close and warm and stinking wonderfully of char, spirits, and crushed reagent. An alchemist's bench runs the length of it — glass coils, a cold burner, racked vials, and a clutter of deep-gathered stuffs no surface bench ever sees: dried moss that glows, ground chitin, a stoppered jar of raw glimmer-dust breathing faint blue light through the glass. This is the working end of Lastlight, where the deep is boiled down into things a delver can carry. Whoever keeps it clearly went adventuring for the ingredients themselves and came back grinning. The common lies back north.

d10.camp.ropehead.name: The Rope-Head
d10.camp.ropehead.description: The east edge of Lastlight, where the lamplight thins and the kept ground gives out at the lip of a black shaft driven down into the rock. This is the jump-off: iron pitons hammered into the throat of the shaft, coils of good line flaked out ready, a windlass half-built over the drop. Below is the deep the whole camp is a staging-post for — the warded way down, and whatever the deep-folk keep their gate shut against. Nobody ropes down alone, and nobody ropes down unmended: a camp healer keeps a post here to send delvers over the edge whole, and to catch what comes back up. The rigging is not yet finished, and the shaft does not yet take a climber. The common lies back west.

d5.fault.deep.name: The Fault's Foot
d5.fault.deep.description: The crack lets you down a long, scraping squeeze through cold rock and opens, all at once, into a chamber with no floor of light at all — the dark here is the deep kind, the kind that drinks a flame and gives nothing back. Dripping water ticks somewhere unseen, and the air is dead and still. The fault you came down by climbs back up; the chamber goes on down, where the rock has split further and the draft rises from somewhere deeper still.

exitMessages.up: You climb the fault's long throat, chimney after scraping chimney, until the draft turns warm and the Riven Yard's grey light opens overhead.

d5.fault.warmvein.name: The Warming Vein
d5.fault.warmvein.description: The fault narrows to a crawl east, and something changes: the dead cold of the chamber behind gives way, degree by degree, to a dry warmth that has no business this deep. The still air begins to stir and move against your face, carrying the smell of hot stone and a faint sulphur bite. Ahead, past a last elbow of rock, a low restless glow washes the walls a dull orange — the first light down here that no one lit. The chamber lies back west; the glow is east.

d6.fault.lavalake.name: The Cinder Pool
d6.fault.lavalake.description: The passage opens onto a low, wide cavern half-filled by a lake of molten rock. It is small as such things go — a stone's throw across — but it seethes, its skin cracking into slow orange veins and healing over black, and it throws a wavering light that reaches every corner of the room. Heat presses on you like a hand. Cinders lift off the surface, drift, and wink out. The rock underfoot is scorched smooth and warm through the boots. The cool way out is back west; the cavern's far side runs on, deeper into the heat.

d11.underhearth.pool.name: The Underhearth
d11.underhearth.pool.description: The shaft's iron rungs end on a warm shelf of scorched rock, and the dark opens into a low vault half-filled by a small lake of molten stone. It is no larger than the croft's own garden, but it seethes — its black skin cracking into slow orange veins and healing over — and throws a steady, wavering red light that needs no lamp. Heat sits on the room like a lid. Whatever the old man above makes of this place, he has left it be: nothing hunts here, nothing stirs but the cinders that lift off the lake and wink out. The rungs climb back up toward the croft. Beyond the lake the vault runs on into unlit dark, where no path has yet been cut.

exitMessages.up: You find the iron rungs again and start the long haul back up, the heat falling away behind you rung by rung, the air cooling and dampening, until the pale lamplight of the study finally opens overhead and you climb out onto the croft floor, four depths above the glow.
